From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/SM_Che%C5%82mno_Ratusz_%284%29_ID_601491.jpg/250px-SM_Che%C5%82mno_Ratusz_%284%29_ID_601491.jpg)](/wiki/File:SM_Che%C5%82mno_Ratusz_(4)_ID_601491.jpg)

The legal constitution takes its name from [Chełmno](/wiki/Che%C5%82mno "Chełmno"), the first town to obtain such law.

**Kulm law**, **Culm law** or **Chełmno Law** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Kulmer Recht*; [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Jus Culmense vetus*; [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *Prawo chełmińskie*) was a legal [constitution](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution") for a [municipal](/wiki/Municipal "Municipal") [form of government](/wiki/Form_of_government "Form of government") used in several [Central European](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") cities in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period").

It was initiated on 28 December 1233 in the [Monastic State of the Teutonic Knights](/wiki/Monastic_State_of_the_Teutonic_Knights "Monastic State of the Teutonic Knights") by [Grand Master](/wiki/Grand_Master_of_the_Teutonic_Order "Grand Master of the Teutonic Order") [Hermann von Salza](/wiki/Hermann_von_Salza "Hermann von Salza") and [Hermann Balk](/wiki/Hermann_Balk "Hermann Balk") when the towns of [Toruń](/wiki/Toru%C5%84 "Toruń") (*Thorn*) and [Chełmno](/wiki/Che%C5%82mno "Chełmno") (*Kulm*) received [German town law](/wiki/German_town_law "German town law"), in particular as a modification of [Magdeburg rights](/wiki/Magdeburg_rights "Magdeburg rights"). Named after the town it was signed in, the original document (*Kulmer Handfeste*) was lost in 1244 when the town hall burned due to an attack by [Świętopełk II, Duke of Pomerania](/wiki/%C5%9Awi%C4%99tope%C5%82k_II,_Duke_of_Pomerania "Świętopełk II, Duke of Pomerania"). The renewed charter of 1 October 1251 was based on a copy in Toruń, but the rights were reduced. The town hall in Chełmno was the seat of the higher court of Chełmno law until 1458.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

This type of law was mostly granted by the [Teutonic Order](/wiki/Teutonic_Order "Teutonic Order") to cities within their monastic state, and by the neighboring [Duchy of Masovia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Masovia "Duchy of Masovia"), but also elsewhere in Poland and [Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania"). The law introduced a number of advantages for residents over Magdeburg rights, including the right to elect the local judge, and the right of inheritance by descendants of both genders.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESokołowski201819-2) From the 13th to the 18th century, at least 224 towns were vested with the law.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESokołowski201819-2) In addition, the Kulm law was expanded, independently from the Knights, to a larger set of laws called *[Alter Kulm](/w/index.php?title=Alter_Kulm&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alter Kulm (page does not exist)")*.

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") the charter of 1251 was [stolen](/wiki/Looting_of_Poland_in_World_War_II "Looting of Poland in World War II") by the [German occupiers](/wiki/Occupation_of_Poland_(1939%E2%80%931945) "Occupation of Poland (1939–1945)") from the [Old Town City Hall in Toruń](/wiki/Old_Town_City_Hall_in_Toru%C5%84 "Old Town City Hall in Toruń"), but was restored to Poland after the war.[[3]](#cite_note-pp-3) It is held in the State Archive in Toruń since 1959.[[3]](#cite_note-pp-3) It is listed on the Polish National List of the [Memory of the World Register](/wiki/Memory_of_the_World_Register "Memory of the World Register"), which includes candidates for the international register.[[3]](#cite_note-pp-3)

## Cities and towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kulm_law&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Cities and towns")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Old_Town_Market_Square_in_Warsaw%2C_2022%2C_01.jpg/250px-Old_Town_Market_Square_in_Warsaw%2C_2022%2C_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Town_Market_Square_in_Warsaw,_2022,_01.jpg)

[Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), largest city which possessed Chełmno Law, whose Old Town is a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") and [Historic Monument of Poland](/wiki/Historic_Monument_(Poland) "Historic Monument (Poland)")

Cities located under Chełmno Law are currently mostly located in northern and central [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), in the regions of [Dobrzyń Land](/wiki/Dobrzy%C5%84_Land "Dobrzyń Land"), [Krajna](/wiki/Krajna "Krajna"), [Kuyavia](/wiki/Kuyavia "Kuyavia"), [Masuria](/wiki/Masuria "Masuria"), [Mazovia](/wiki/Mazovia "Mazovia"), [Podlachia](/wiki/Podlachia "Podlachia"),[[4]](#cite_note-wj-4) [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania"), [Powiśle](/wiki/Powi%C5%9Ble_(region) "Powiśle (region)"), [Warmia](/wiki/Warmia "Warmia"), with some in southern Poland ([Lesser Poland](/wiki/Lesser_Poland "Lesser Poland")), and in [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") and [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") ([Kaliningrad Oblast](/wiki/Kaliningrad_Oblast "Kaliningrad Oblast")). The largest are Polish capital [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), major cities on the [Vistula River](/wiki/Vistula_River "Vistula River") [Płock](/wiki/P%C5%82ock "Płock"), [Toruń](/wiki/Toru%C5%84 "Toruń") and [Grudziądz](/wiki/Grudzi%C4%85dz "Grudziądz"), major port cities of [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk"), [Kaliningrad](/wiki/Kaliningrad "Kaliningrad") and [Klaipėda](/wiki/Klaip%C4%97da "Klaipėda"), plus [Olsztyn](/wiki/Olsztyn "Olsztyn").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Torun_rynek_staromiejski_%282%29.jpg/250px-Torun_rynek_staromiejski_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Torun_rynek_staromiejski_(2).jpg)

Old Town of [Toruń](/wiki/Toru%C5%84 "Toruń"), UNESCO World Heritage Site and Historic Monument of Poland

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/DJI_0838-Enhanced-NR-HDR-Edit.jpg/250px-DJI_0838-Enhanced-NR-HDR-Edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:DJI_0838-Enhanced-NR-HDR-Edit.jpg)

Cathedral Hill in [Płock](/wiki/P%C5%82ock "Płock") with the [castle](/wiki/P%C5%82ock_Castle "Płock Castle") and [cathedral](/wiki/P%C5%82ock_Cathedral "Płock Cathedral") containing a number of sarcophagi of Polish monarchs, [Historic Monument of Poland](/wiki/Historic_Monument_(Poland) "Historic Monument (Poland)")

The historic centers of Toruń and Warsaw are listed as [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"),[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) with the historic centers of [Chełmno](/wiki/Che%C5%82mno "Chełmno"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk")[[8]](#cite_note-8) and [Tykocin](/wiki/Tykocin "Tykocin"),[[9]](#cite_note-9) and major landmarks of [Czerwińsk nad Wisłą](/wiki/Czerwi%C5%84sk_nad_Wis%C5%82%C4%85 "Czerwińsk nad Wisłą"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Grudziądz](/wiki/Grudzi%C4%85dz "Grudziądz"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) [Kwidzyn](/wiki/Kwidzyn "Kwidzyn"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Lidzbark Warmiński](/wiki/Lidzbark_Warmi%C5%84ski "Lidzbark Warmiński"),[[13]](#cite_note-13) [Malbork](/wiki/Malbork "Malbork"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Olsztyn](/wiki/Olsztyn "Olsztyn"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Płock](/wiki/P%C5%82ock "Płock")[[16]](#cite_note-16) and [Pułtusk](/wiki/Pu%C5%82tusk "Pułtusk")[[17]](#cite_note-17) additionally listed as [Historic Monuments of Poland](/wiki/Historic_Monument_(Poland) "Historic Monument (Poland)").

Old Town of [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk"), Historic Monument of Poland
